summary: Gruber reiterates his skepticism about Google's Duplex demo recordings, arguing that Google has only shown pre-recorded calls rather than allowing anyone to witness a live demonstration. He notes that Sundar Pichai's stage presentation strongly implied Duplex works today as heard, yet Google has stonewalled press inquiries and refused to let journalists see it operate in real time. Gruber contrasts this with Apple's 2007 iPhone unveiling, where prototype hardware was demoed live and handed to reporters. He argues that a recording is not a demo, and that journalists should have reported Google's claims with appropriate skepticism rather than present tense certainty.
key_points: Google has only released recordings of Duplex calls but has never allowed journalists to witness a live call or interact with the system directly
Pichai's on-stage framing strongly implied Duplex works today as demonstrated, and media coverage universally reported it in the present tense without qualification
Even a behind-the-scenes press preview at Google's campus only played recordings, not live demonstrations
Apple's 2007 iPhone keynote serves as the gold standard for honest demoing — live on stage, then hands-on with press, even with unfinished software
Headlines should have said Google 'claims' Assistant can make human-sounding calls, reflecting that nothing was independently verified
money_quotes: A recording is not a demo.
It's like a card trick where the magician, rather than an audience member, picks the card and shuffles the deck.

The headlines last week should have been along the lines of 'Google Claims Assistant Can Make Human-Sounding Phone Calls', not 'Google Assistant Can Make Human-Sounding Phone Calls'. There's a difference.
The job of journalists is to verify these things, not just to take a company's word for it.
But on the other hand, if they are that close, why haven't they invited anyone from the media to see Duplex in action?
Part of the reason I'm so focused on Duplex is that if it really works like it does in these recordings, it's one of the most amazing advances in technology in years.
That's how you prove to the world that a demo was what you said it was. It is damn curious that Google won't do that with Duplex.
key_insight: A recording presented as a demo is an unverifiable claim, not evidence, and both tech companies and the journalists covering them have an obligation to make that distinction clear.
